text: The surgeon performs medial dissection down to the pubic tubercle, colon, and Cooper's ligament, noting the ease of dissection due to the patient's peritoneum thickness.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection to reduce tension on the flap and prevent inadvertent tearing of the peritoneum during the robotic left direct inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon identifies the large direct hernia sac and begins the reduction process, ensuring careful manipulation to avoid pulling the bladder into the sac, especially when using cautery.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection in the preperitoneal space, noting the absence of an indirect sac, which facilitates easier dissection.

The surgeon performs inferior dissection below the obturator space, revealing the typical fat plug. Final lateral dissection is carried out to create adequate space for the placement of a 12x16 ProGrip mesh, ensuring good inferior coverage due to the direct effect.

The surgeon identifies a lipoma hidden beneath wispy transverse fibers and carefully clears the area to reveal it. The lipoma is brought down, ensuring the testicle is not inadvertently reduced, and the surgeon decides to resect the lipoma.
The surgeon examines the final dissection area and performs additional lateral dissection to ensure proper placement of the mesh.

The surgeon performs an invocation of the pseudosec, involving the attenuated transversal fascia, securing it down to Cooper's ligament. This technique is intended to decrease seroma formation and provide a scaffold for the mesh, preventing eventration into the large direct defect.

The surgeon centers the mesh slightly more laterally than usual for a large indirect defect. Using a remnant suture from the imbrication of the direct sac, the surgeon fixates the mesh medially to prevent early migration in the area of weakness.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap, running sutures multiple times to secure it. An angiocath is used to evacuate gas from the peritoneal flap, ensuring the mesh lies flat beneath it.
